memes.label: "The Social Contract"
memes.description: "Structural hub linking to all four Books of Rousseau's The Social Contract"

memes.label: Aristocracy
memes.description: A form of government in which authority is vested in a small number of citizens, analyzed by Rousseau as the best practicable form when elective.

memes.label: Body Politic
memes.description: Rousseau's concept of the body politic as a moral and collective being created by the social compact — a public person with its own will, life, and unity distinct from the individuals who compose it

memes.label: Caligula
memes.description: Caligula, the Roman emperor whose reign of arbitrary cruelty Rousseau cites in The Social Contract as the archetype of despotic rule — the logical endpoint of political degeneration when sovereignty is usurped by a single will

memes.label: Censorship
memes.description: Rousseau's concept of censorship not as a tribunal over opinion but as the guardian of public morals — an institution that preserves manners and opinion, which Rousseau considered more important than laws themselves

memes.label: Civil Liberty
memes.description: Rousseau's concept of the liberty gained by entering civil society — freedom under self-imposed law, secured by the social compact against personal dependence

memes.label: Civil Religion
memes.description: Rousseau's doctrine that the State requires a civil religion — a set of civic sentiments and duties that bind citizens to the community, distinct from both theological dogma and private conscience

memes.label: Comitia
memes.description: The Roman Comitia, the popular assemblies of the Roman Republic that Rousseau analyzes as an exemplar of how a sovereign people can exercise its legislative power through institutional forms

memes.label: Decline of Rome
memes.description: The decline of the Roman Republic, which Rousseau analyzes as the paradigmatic case of governmental usurpation of sovereignty — the process by which the general will is replaced by particular interests

memes.label: Democracy
memes.description: A form of government in which the sovereign entrusts executive power to the whole people or a majority — regarded by Rousseau as an ideal never realized and suited only to small, virtuous states.

memes.label: England
memes.description: England, frequently referenced in Rousseau's The Social Contract as a counterexample — a nation whose people mistakenly believe themselves free under a representative system that Rousseau argues is a form of slavery punctuated by electoral moments

memes.label: Equality
memes.description: Rousseau's conception of equality as a fundamental condition of legitimate political society — not absolute equality of wealth and power, but equality before the law and the absence of personal dependence

memes.label: Executive Power
memes.description: The physical force of the body politic that executes the general will through particular acts — distinct from the legislative power (the will) and vested in the government or prince.

memes.label: Foundation of the Roman Republic
memes.description: The establishment of the Roman Republic following the expulsion of the Tarquin kings — a foundational moment Rousseau analyzes as exemplifying the transition from monarchy to legitimate popular sovereignty through constitutional founding

memes.label: Founding of Rome
memes.description: The legendary founding of Rome, which Rousseau discusses as an example of how a new political community is constituted — the act by which a multitude becomes a people with a common identity and purpose

memes.label: France
memes.description: France, the primary political context of Rousseau's critique — the archetype of a corrupt modern state where inequality and absolutism have destroyed legitimate political order

memes.label: French Absolutism
memes.description: The consolidation of absolute monarchy in France under Louis XIV, representing for Rousseau the complete corruption of legitimate political order — where the will of one replaces the general will

memes.label: French Parlement
memes.description: The French Parlements, the sovereign courts of the ancien régime that Rousseau criticized as usurping legislative authority under the guise of judicial review — an example of the confusion of sovereignty with government

memes.label: General Will
memes.description: Rousseau's concept of the general will (volonté générale) as the collective will of the sovereign people directed toward the common good — the central principle of The Social Contract

memes.label: Geneva
memes.description: Geneva, Rousseau's birthplace and the model republic whose institutions shaped his political thought. The Social Contract is dedicated 'To the Republic of Geneva.'

memes.label: Genevan General Council
memes.description: The General Council of the Republic of Geneva, the assembly of all citizens that exercised sovereign legislative power — the living model of popular sovereignty that shaped Rousseau's political thought

memes.label: Genevan Reformation
memes.description: The establishment of the Reformed Republic of Geneva in the 16th century, which created the Calvinist city-state that Rousseau would later cite as a model of popular sovereignty

memes.label: Government
memes.description: Rousseau's distinction between sovereignty (legislative will) and government (executive power) — government as the commissioned agent of the Sovereign, not the Sovereign itself

memes.label: Hugo Grotius
memes.description: Hugo Grotius (1583–1645), Dutch jurist and philosopher whose De Jure Belli ac Pacis (1625) founded modern natural law theory — a major target of Rousseau's critique in the opening chapters of The Social Contract

memes.label: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
memes.description: Jean-Jacques Rousseau (1712–1778), Genevan philosopher and author of The Social Contract, whose political philosophy profoundly shaped modern democratic and republican thought

memes.label: John Locke
memes.description: John Locke (1632–1704), English philosopher whose Two Treatises of Government (1689) articulated a social contract theory based on natural rights — an influence on Rousseau that Rousseau also sharply criticizes

memes.label: Law
memes.description: Rousseau's doctrine that laws are acts of the general will — general in their source and general in their object — forming the legitimate framework of civil society

memes.label: Legislator
memes.description: In Rousseau's The Social Contract, the Legislator is the extraordinary figure who gives a people its fundamental constitution — distinct from both the sovereign who makes laws and the magistrate who executes them

memes.label: Legislation of Lycurgus
memes.description: The legislation of Lycurgus for Sparta — the archetypal act of the great Legislator in Rousseau's political theory, who transformed a people by giving them institutions that shaped their character

memes.label: Magistrate
memes.description: In Rousseau's The Social Contract, magistrates are the officers or "kings" entrusted with the exercise of executive power — distinct from the sovereign people who alone hold legislative authority

memes.label: Monarchy
memes.description: A form of government in which executive power is concentrated in the hands of a single magistrate — suited to great states but, Rousseau argues, inherently inferior to republican government.

memes.label: Montesquieu
memes.description: Montesquieu (1689–1755), French political philosopher whose The Spirit of the Laws (1748) profoundly influenced Rousseau's theory of law, government, and the relationship between political institutions and social conditions

memes.label: Natural Liberty
memes.description: Rousseau's concept of the unlimited but precarious freedom of the state of nature — the right to everything one can obtain, which is surrendered in exchange for civil liberty under the social compact

memes.label: Persia
memes.description: Persia, the archetype of Oriental despotism in Rousseau's references — a vast empire whose size, in Rousseau's analysis, made monarchy the only viable form of government but which also demonstrated the corrupting tendency of absolute rule

memes.label: Popular Assemblies
memes.description: The popular assemblies (comitia) of Rome — the institutional mechanism through which the Roman people exercised sovereignty by electing magistrates, passing laws, and judging cases, serving as Rousseau's primary historical model of legitimate popular government

memes.label: Property
memes.description: Rousseau's theory that property is established by the social compact, transforming precarious natural possession into a secure civil right grounded in law rather than force

memes.label: Roman Curiae
memes.description: The curiae, Rome's oldest political divisions rooted in the original three tribes, which Rousseau discusses in Book IV as the foundational layer of Rome's constitutional architecture — the earliest form through which the Roman people organized for political and religious purposes

memes.label: Roman Expansion
memes.description: The territorial expansion of Rome from city-state to Mediterranean empire — a process Rousseau analyzes in The Social Contract as illustrating both the sources of Roman greatness and the structural forces that ultimately destroyed the Republic

memes.label: Roman Senate
memes.description: The Roman Senate, the aristocratic council of the Roman Republic that Rousseau discusses as an example of government — an intermediate body between the sovereign people and the magistrates

memes.label: Rome
memes.description: Rome, the exemplar par excellence in Rousseau's political theory — both as the model of a well-constituted republic and as a cautionary tale of imperial decline

memes.label: Servius Tullius
memes.description: Servius Tullius, the sixth legendary king of Rome, whom Rousseau cites as the architect of the centuriate organization — a model of constitutional reform that distributed political power by property rather than birth

memes.label: Slavery
memes.description: A condition of absolute subjection that Rousseau systematically argues is illegitimate, absurd, and contrary to natural right — the right of slavery is "null and void" because the concepts of slave and right are mutually exclusive.

memes.label: Social Compact
memes.description: Rousseau's foundational concept of the agreement by which individuals unite to form a political community, creating the legitimate basis for sovereignty and civil society

memes.label: Sovereignty
memes.description: Rousseau's doctrine that sovereignty is the exercise of the general will, inalienable and indivisible, residing in the people as a collective body

memes.label: Sparta
memes.description: Sparta, the ancient Greek city-state Rousseau admires as the achievement of a great legislator — Lycurgus — who gave it laws and institutions that shaped its citizens' character

memes.label: Spartan Gerousia
memes.description: The Spartan Gerousia, the council of elders in ancient Sparta that formed part of the mixed constitution Rousseau admired — an aristocratic element balancing the popular assembly and the kings

memes.label: State of Nature
memes.description: Rousseau's concept of the pre-political condition of humanity — a state without law, government, or moral relations — which the social compact is designed to transcend

memes.label: Thomas Hobbes
memes.description: Thomas Hobbes (1588–1679), English philosopher whose Leviathan (1651) founded modern social contract theory — Rousseau's primary foil in developing his own distinct doctrine of the compact

memes.label: Tribunate
memes.description: Rousseau's concept of the tribunate as a special magistracy — a preservative institution that stands between the Sovereign and the government, protecting the laws and the legislative power without sharing in either

memes.label: Venice
memes.description: Venice, the maritime republic Rousseau sometimes cites as an example of a well-constituted government — though his admiration is qualified and primarily directed at its institutional structure

memes.label: Will of All
memes.description: Rousseau's concept of the will of all (volonté de tous) as the mere aggregate of private interests — distinct from the general will, which considers only the common interest
